children.name: Sitta
children.description: The only birds that routinely walk headfirst down tree trunks, gripping bark with powerful feet and using the bill to lever up bark and extract insects. They wedge nuts in bark crevices to hack them open — hence the name. The Eurasian Nuthatch plasters the entrance of its nest hole with mud to exactly its own body diameter.

children.name: Sitta europaea
children.description: The Eurasian nuthatch, wood nuthatch, or simply nuthatch, is a small passerine bird found throughout the Palearctic and in Europe. Like other nuthatches, it is a short-tailed bird with a long bill, blue-gray upperparts and a black eye-stripe. It is a vocal bird with a repeated loud dwip call.

children.name: Sitta arctica
children.description: A large pale nuthatch of eastern Siberian larch forests, formerly considered a subspecies of the Eurasian Nuthatch. It has a white belly and undertail, with a black eyestripe that extends onto the nape.

children.name: Sitta nagaensis
children.description: A small nuthatch of montane forests from northeast India to Vietnam, with a white face and chestnut-red flanks and vent. It forages actively in mixed-species flocks across pine and oak woodlands.

children.name: Sitta cashmirensis
children.description: A slim, long-billed nuthatch of Himalayan forests from Afghanistan to Nepal, with unstreaked pale grey underparts and a broad black eyestripe. It nests in tree cavities lined with bark flakes and hair.

children.name: Sitta castanea
children.description: A richly coloured nuthatch of Indian lowland forests, the male with deep chestnut underparts and a white face. It frequently joins mixed-species foraging flocks and has a loud, penetrating call.

children.name: Sitta cinnamoventris
children.description: Formerly grouped with the Chestnut-bellied Nuthatch, this species ranges from the eastern Himalayas to Thailand and Vietnam, distinguished by browner upperparts and a cinnamon wash on the belly.

children.name: Sitta neglecta
children.description: A small, pale nuthatch of dry forests in Myanmar, Laos, Thailand, and Cambodia, with a narrow black eyestripe and buff-white underparts.

children.name: Sitta himalayensis
children.description: A small nuthatch of Himalayan and Southeast Asian montane forests, distinguished by white patches at the base of the tail feathers. It has a distinctive nasal call and feeds in the mid-storey on insects and larvae.

children.name: Sitta frontalis
children.description: A brilliantly coloured nuthatch with a violet-blue back, salmon-pink underparts, and a striking black patch on the forehead. It ranges from Sri Lanka and India through Southeast Asia to Indonesia. Its rapid, high-pitched calls are a hallmark of Asian lowland forests.

children.name: Sitta azurea
children.description: A spectacular nuthatch of Southeast Asian montane forests, with azure-blue upperparts, a black mask, and pale grey underparts. It is more often heard than seen, with a distinctive metallic call.

children.name: Sitta pygmaea
children.description: A tiny, gregarious nuthatch of western North American pine forests, often found in noisy flocks of up to 100 birds. It roosts communally in tree cavities, sometimes with dozens of birds packed into a single hole on cold winter nights.

children.name: Sitta pusilla
children.description: A small, vocal nuthatch of the southeastern US pine forests, with a distinctive nasal call that sounds like a toy rubber duck. It uses a bark scale as a tool to pry up other bark flaps when foraging — one of the few birds to use tools.

children.name: Sitta insularis
children.description: A critically endangered nuthatch endemic to Grand Bahama Island, with a distinctive white stripe above the eyestripe. Its population was devastated by Hurricane Matthew in 2016 and Dorian in 2019; fewer than 50 individuals may remain.

children.name: Sitta carolinensis
children.description: The largest North American nuthatch, a common and familiar bird of deciduous and mixed woodlands. Its nasal 'yank-yank' call is a familiar sound at feeders across the continent. It has a striking black cap and white face with no eyestripe.

children.name: Sitta canadensis
children.description: A small, active nuthatch of North American coniferous forests, with a rusty breast, black eyestripe, and white stripe above. It smears tree resin around its nest-hole entrance — a behaviour thought to deter predators and competitors.

children.name: Sitta whiteheadi
children.description: A small nuthatch endemic to the mountain pine forests of Corsica, where it is the island's only endemic bird species. It is restricted to old-growth Corsican pine stands above 1000 m and is vulnerable to forest fires. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Sitta ledanti
children.description: A small nuthatch restricted to a few mountain forests in northeastern Algeria, discovered only in 1975. It is one of the most range-restricted bird species in the Palearctic, confined to ancient Algerian oak forests. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Sitta krueperi
children.description: A small nuthatch of Turkish and Aegean coniferous forests with a distinctive red breast patch and black crown. Named after German ornithologist Hans Krüper. It is declining due to habitat fragmentation. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Sitta villosa
children.description: A small nuthatch of Chinese and Korean temperate forests, with a distinctive white supercilium extending well behind the eye. It is a frequent associate of mixed-species winter flocks and has a high-pitched trilling song.

children.name: Sitta yunnanensis
children.description: A small nuthatch endemic to the pine forests of Yunnan in southwestern China. It has a narrow black eyestripe and white cheeks, and is often found in pairs or small family groups. IUCN status: Near Threatened.

children.name: Sitta neumayer
children.description: A nuthatch adapted to rocky, treeless habitats of the Balkans, Turkey, and the Caucasus. It builds a distinctive flask-shaped mud nest cemented to a rock face with a narrow entrance tube. Unlike tree-nesting nuthatches, it does not use mud for nest defense.

children.name: Sitta tephronota
children.description: A large, pale rock nuthatch of Central Asia, Iran, and the Caucasus, nesting in clay-like mud structures on cliffs and rocks. It is similar to the Western Rock Nuthatch but larger and paler, with stronger song differences.

children.name: Sitta magna
children.description: The world's largest nuthatch, at 19 cm — almost twice the size of the Eurasian Nuthatch. It inhabits open pine forests of Myanmar, Thailand, and southwestern China. Its population is declining due to habitat loss. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Sitta formosa
children.description: A strikingly patterned nuthatch of the eastern Himalayas and Southeast Asian highlands, with a black-and-white striped head, bright blue back, and rich chestnut flanks. It is rare throughout its range and closely tied to mature broadleaf forests. IUCN status: Vulnerable.

children.name: Sitta victoriae
children.description: A small nuthatch endemic to the Mount Victoria area of western Myanmar, where it is confined to mature oak and pine forests above 2000 m. It is one of the most range-restricted Asian nuthatches. IUCN status: Endangered.

children.name: Sitta solangiae
children.description: A mid-sized nuthatch of montane forests in Vietnam, Laos, and Hainan, with a distinctive yellow-orange bill and white underparts. Deforestation across its range has led to population declines.

children.name: Sitta oenochlamys
children.description: A colourful nuthatch endemic to the Philippine islands, with a bright yellow-orange bill, deep rufous underparts, and a violet-blue back. It is a canopy specialist, foraging high in the crowns of lowland and montane forests for insects.

children.name: Sitta leucopsis
children.description: A nuthatch of the western Himalayas with white cheeks, a black cap, and pale orange-buff underparts. It inhabits temperate coniferous and mixed forests from Pakistan to Nepal, often foraging in mixed-species flocks.

children.name: Sitta przewalskii
children.description: A rare nuthatch of southeastern Tibet and western China, formerly considered a subspecies of the White-cheeked Nuthatch. It is found in remote mountain forests and is poorly known.
